title: Mechanism of Action as Pharmacological Primitive
subtitle: When Schank Meets Drug Discovery: Decomposing Pharmacology into Primitive Operators

children.content: The Broad Repurposing Hub's 1,135 mechanism-of-action classes decompose drug action the same way Schank decomposed event semantics. 'Ciprofloxacin treats infection' decomposes into INHIBIT(agent=ciprofloxacin, target=DNA_gyrase, effect=bacterial_death) — a Conceptual Dependency structure with a primitive operator. Schankian primitives aren't specific to human action — they're a universal vocabulary for state transitions in ANY system.

children.title: The Parallel: Events and Drug Actions

children.content: In 1975, Roger Schank proposed that all human actions decompose into ~14 primitive operators: ATRANS (transfer of possession), PTRANS (physical transfer), MTRANS (mental transfer), INGEST (taking in), and so on. Any complex event — 'John mailed a letter' — decomposes into a composition of primitives: ATRANS(possession, letter, John→post_office) ∘ PTRANS(location, letter, house→post_office).
Now look at pharmacology. The Broad Institute's Repurposing Hub catalogs 6,798 drugs with 1,135 mechanism-of-action classes. Every drug action decomposes similarly:

Natural language: 'Ampicillin kills bacteria'
Pharmacological decomposition: INHIBIT(agent=ampicillin, target=PBP, effect=cell_wall_disruption) → KILL(target=bacterium)

Natural language: 'Ciprofloxacin treats UTI'
Pharmacological decomposition: INHIBIT(agent=ciprofloxacin, target=DNA_gyrase, effect=replication_halt) → KILL(target=E_coli) → RESOLVE(condition=UTI)

Natural language: 'Ethambutol treats tuberculosis'
Pharmacological decomposition: INHIBIT(agent=ethambutol, target=arabinosyltransferase, effect=cell_wall_synthesis_blocked) → KILL(target=M_tuberculosis)

The structure is identical to Schank's Conceptual Dependency: an OPERATOR applied to an AGENT, TARGET, and EFFECT. The 1,135 MoA classes are pharmacology's equivalent of Schank's primitives — just not yet compressed to their minimal basis.
If we apply wake-sleep compression (the same algorithm from our operator discovery paper) to the 1,135 MoA classes, we'd expect to rediscover a small basis set of pharmacological primitives:
INHIBIT

Block an enzyme, receptor, or pathway. The most common primitive — covers ~60% of all drug MoAs. Analogous to Schank's ATRANS (removing capability from target). Examples: kinase inhibitors, protease inhibitors, cell wall synthesis inhibitors.
ACTIVATE
Stimulate a receptor or pathway. The inverse of INHIBIT. Analogous to Schank's PROPEL (causing action). Examples: adrenergic agonists, dopamine agonists.
MODULATE

Adjust the sensitivity or gain of a system without directly activating or inhibiting. Analogous to Schank's ATTEND (changing processing state). Examples: allosteric modulators, ion channel modulators.
DISRUPT
Physically damage or destabilize a structure. No direct Schank analog — this is a destruction primitive. Examples: membrane disruptors, DNA intercalators, microtubule destabilizers.
MIMIC
Substitute for an endogenous molecule. Analogous to Schank's MBUILD (constructing a representation). Examples: nucleoside analogs, hormone replacement, substrate mimics.

Prediction: Wake-sleep compression on the 1,135 MoA annotations should converge on 5–8 pharmacological primitives, analogous to the 4–6 Schankian primitives we rediscovered from ATOMIC event descriptions. The description length (MDL) of the compressed library should be within ~5% of the hand-crafted pharmacology textbook decomposition.

children.title: Compound Operators: Combination Therapy as Operator Composition

children.content: One of the most striking findings from our Schankian operator work was compound operators: 'mail' = ATRANS ∘ PTRANS, 'trade' = ATRANS ∘ ATRANS. Pharmacology has an exact parallel: combination therapy.

The algebra is the same. Pharmacologists discovered empirically what Schank formalized theoretically: complex actions decompose into compositions of a small set of primitives. The compositionality is what makes both systems predictive — you can design new combinations by reasoning about operator compositions.

children.title: Why This Matters: Scaffold Hopping as Operator Preservation

children.content: In our HRR antibiotic discovery pipeline, we encoded 6,798 drugs as high-dimensional vectors capturing both molecular structure AND mechanism of action. When we performed scaffold-hopping (finding new molecular structures that preserve activity), we were implicitly searching for molecules that preserve the operator while changing the argument.

In language: 'John gave Mary the book' → 'Mary received the book from John' preserves the ATRANS operator while changing the surface syntax.

In pharmacology: Ethambutol → [Novel diamine analog] preserves the INHIBIT(arabinosyltransferase) operator while changing the molecular scaffold.

Both are operator-preserving transformations over different surface representations. The HRR vector algebra makes this precise: the operator component of the vector is preserved while the structural component varies.

children.title: The Pharmacophore IS the Operator Signature

children.content: A pharmacophore — the spatial arrangement of features necessary for biological activity — is exactly the 'operator signature' in molecular space. Our analysis of the ethambutol cluster found the pharmacophore to be: small branched aliphatic diamine, no aromatic rings, MW < 250, ≥2 nitrogen atoms. This is the structural motif that encodes the INHIBIT(arabinosyltransferase) operator. Any molecule matching this pharmacophore implements the same primitive, regardless of the specific atoms used.

This connects to a deep idea: in Schank's theory, the primitive IS the abstraction that's preserved across paraphrases. In pharmacology, the pharmacophore IS the abstraction preserved across scaffold hops. They're the same mathematical object — an equivalence class under operator-preserving transformations.

The deepest implication: Schankian primitives aren't a theory of human action specifically. They're a theory of state transitions in complex systems. Any domain where agents cause changes to objects through mechanisms will converge on a similar small operator vocabulary:

The convergence isn't coincidental. Any system with bounded description length will compress its action vocabulary to a minimal basis — that's the minimum description length (MDL) principle. Schank found the human-action basis. Pharmacology found the drug-action basis. Our wake-sleep compression algorithm rediscovers both from data.

questions.question: Why do the Broad Repurposing Hub's 1,135 MoA classes resemble Schank's primitive operators?
options: Both decompose complex actions into a small set of reusable primitives
They were designed by the same researchers
They both use lambda calculus notation
It's a coincidence with no deeper connection
questions.explanation: Both systems independently converge on the same insight: complex actions in any domain decompose into compositions of a small primitive vocabulary. This is the MDL principle at work — bounded description length forces compression to a minimal basis.

questions.question: What is the pharmacological equivalent of Schank's compound operator 'mail = ATRANS ∘ PTRANS'?
options: A single drug with one mechanism
A drug that has no known mechanism
A drug that works on multiple species
questions.explanation: Combination therapy composes multiple primitive operators, just as compound events compose multiple Schankian primitives. Augmentin combines two INHIBIT operators targeting different enzymes, analogous to 'mail' combining ATRANS and PTRANS.

questions.question: In scaffold hopping, what stays constant while the molecular structure changes?
questions.explanation: The pharmacophore — the spatial arrangement of features encoding the mechanism of action — is the 'operator' that's preserved. Just as linguistic paraphrases preserve the Schankian primitive while changing surface syntax, scaffold hops preserve the pharmacophore while changing the molecular scaffold.
